---
title: "Dynamic Choice and IRL, Visually"
subtitle: "Definitions, algorithms, diagnostics, and counterfactuals"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    fragments: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #111214;
  --fg: #f1f0ea;
  --muted: #9b9c9e;
  --sub: #c9c6bd;
  --rule: #3b3f45;
  --panel: #1b1d20;
  --panel2: #202328;
  --cyan: #63d7d4;
  --blue: #8ab4ff;
  --amber: #f0c674;
  --red: #ff7d7d;
  --green: #98d68a;
  --violet: #c6a0f6;
}
.reveal,
.reveal .slides section {
  background: var(--bg);
  color: var(--fg);
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif;
}
.reveal .slides section { text-align: left; }
.reveal h1,
.reveal h2,
.reveal h3 {
  color: var(--fg);
  font-weight: 680;
  letter-spacing: 0;
  text-transform: none;
}
.reveal h2 {
  font-size: 1.17em;
  line-height: 1.08;
  border-left: 6px solid var(--cyan);
  padding-left: 0.36em;
  margin-bottom: 0.38em;
}
.reveal h3 {
  font-size: 0.76em;
  color: var(--sub);
  margin: 0 0 0.25em 0;
}
.reveal p,
.reveal li {
  font-size: 0.68em;
  line-height: 1.25;
}
.reveal strong { color: var(--cyan); }
.reveal em { color: var(--sub); font-style: italic; }
.reveal code {
  color: var(--green);
  background: rgba(152, 214, 138, 0.1);
  padding: 1px 5px;
  border-radius: 3px;
}
.reveal pre code {
  font-size: 0.62em;
  line-height: 1.28;
  max-height: 55vh;
}
.reveal .slide-number {
  background: none;
  color: var(--muted);
  font-size: 12px;
}
.kicker {
  color: var(--muted);
  font: 700 0.44em ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, Consolas, monospace;
  letter-spacing: 0.08em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-bottom: 0.28em;
}
.lead {
  font-size: 1em;
  line-height: 1.32;
  max-width: 980px;
}
.tight p,
.tight li { margin: 0.22em 0; }
.small p,
.small li { font-size: 0.56em; }
.mini { font-size: 0.52em; color: var(--sub); }
.mono {
  font-family: ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, Consolas, monospace;
  color: #dce7df;
}
.twocol {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 0.55em;
  align-items: stretch;
}
.threecol {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
  gap: 0.46em;
}
.fourcol {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr);
  gap: 0.38em;
}
.stack { display: grid; gap: 0.42em; }
.defbox,
.assumbox,
.propbox,
.claimbox,
.counterbox,
.algobox,
.evidence,
.meaning,
.eqbox,
.card {
  background: var(--panel);
  border: 1.4px solid var(--rule);
  border-radius: 4px;
  padding: 0.44em 0.58em;
}
.defbox { border-left: 5px solid var(--cyan); }
.assumbox { border-left: 5px solid var(--amber); }
.propbox { border-left: 5px solid var(--green); }
.claimbox { border-left: 5px solid var(--blue); }
.counterbox { border-left: 5px solid var(--red); }
.algobox { border-left: 5px solid var(--violet); }
.evidence { border-left: 5px solid var(--blue); }
.meaning {
  border-left: 5px solid var(--muted);
  color: var(--sub);
}
.tag {
  display: block;
  color: var(--muted);
  font: 750 0.44em ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, Consolas, monospace;
  letter-spacing: 0.08em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-bottom: 0.25em;
}
.eqbox {
  width: 92%;
  margin: 0.25em auto;
  text-align: center;
}
.eqbox .math.display { margin: 0.08em 0; }
.reveal .eqbox p { margin: 0.06em 0; }
.mathgrid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 0.44em;
}
.mathgrid .eqbox { width: auto; }
.resulttable {
  width: 94%;
  margin: 0.3em auto;
  border-collapse: collapse;
  font-size: 0.52em;
}
.resulttable th {
  color: var(--cyan);
  font-weight: 720;
  border-bottom: 1.4px solid var(--rule);
  padding: 0.36em 0.42em;
}
.resulttable td {
  color: var(--fg);
  border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);
  padding: 0.36em 0.42em;
  text-align: center;
}
.resulttable td:first-child,
.resulttable th:first-child {
  color: var(--sub);
  text-align: left;
}
.figimg {
  display: block;
  width: 100%;
  max-height: 54vh;
  object-fit: contain;
  margin: 0 auto;
  border-radius: 4px;
}
.figimg.tall { max-height: 60vh; }
.figimg.short { max-height: 47vh; }
.caption {
  color: var(--muted);
  font-size: 0.5em;
  line-height: 1.22;
  text-align: center;
  margin-top: 0.25em;
}
.viz {
  width: 100%;
  height: auto;
  max-height: 56vh;
  display: block;
  margin: 0 auto;
}
.viz.tall { max-height: 62vh; }
.svgpanel {
  fill: var(--panel);
  stroke: var(--rule);
  stroke-width: 1.4;
  rx: 4;
}
.svgpanel.cyan { stroke: var(--cyan); }
.svgpanel.blue { stroke: var(--blue); }
.svgpanel.amber { stroke: var(--amber); }
.svgpanel.red { stroke: var(--red); }
.svgpanel.green { stroke: var(--green); }
.node {
  fill: #171a1e;
  stroke: var(--cyan);
  stroke-width: 2.2;
}
.node.blue { stroke: var(--blue); }
.node.amber { stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.red { stroke: var(--red); }
.node.green { stroke: var(--green); }
.node.violet { stroke: var(--violet); }
.edge {
  stroke: #8b929b;
  stroke-width: 2.1;
  fill: none;
  marker-end: url(#arrow);
}
.edge.cyan { stroke: var(--cyan); marker-end: url(#arrowCyan); }
.edge.blue { stroke: var(--blue); marker-end: url(#arrowBlue); }
.edge.amber { stroke: var(--amber); marker-end: url(#arrowAmber); }
.edge.red { stroke: var(--red); marker-end: url(#arrowRed); }
.dash { stroke-dasharray: 7 5; }
.svgtxt {
  fill: var(--fg);
  font: 640 17px -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", sans-serif;
  text-anchor: middle;
}
.svgtxt.left { text-anchor: start; }
.svgtxt.small { font-size: 13px; font-weight: 600; }
.svgtxt.big { font-size: 24px; font-weight: 720; }
.svgmono {
  fill: #dce7df;
  font: 650 14px ui-monospace, "SF Mono", Menlo, Consolas, monospace;
  text-anchor: middle;
}
.svgmono.left { text-anchor: start; }
.svgnote {
  fill: var(--sub);
  font: 14px -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", sans-serif;
  text-anchor: middle;
}
.svgnote.left { text-anchor: start; }
.axis { stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 2; }
.grid { stroke: #343941; stroke-width: 1; }
.sep {
  display: grid;
  place-items: center;
  min-height: 55vh;
  text-align: center;
}
.sep .mono {
  font-size: 0.76em;
  color: var(--cyan);
}
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.4" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#8b929b"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowCyan" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.4" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#63d7d4"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowBlue" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.4" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#8ab4ff"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowAmber" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.4" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#f0c674"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowRed" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.4" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#ff7d7d"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

## Problem: inverse control from trajectories

::: {.lead}
Given trajectories of states and actions, infer the reward, policy, and value objects that make those trajectories rational under a dynamic decision model.
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 390" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="60" y="82" width="235" height="210"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="178" y="130">observed</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="178" y="182">(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="178" y="232">sample paths</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 306 190 L 430 190"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="445" y="82" width="250" height="210"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="570" y="130">inverse map</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="570" y="182">D -> (r, pi, V)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="570" y="232">model plus assumptions</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 706 190 L 825 190"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="840" y="82" width="110" height="210"/>
  <text class="svgtxt" x="895" y="145">valid</text>
  <text class="svgtxt" x="895" y="175">counter-</text>
  <text class="svgtxt" x="895" y="205">factuals</text>
  <path class="edge red dash" d="M 570 292 C 570 350 250 350 178 295"/>
  <text class="svgnote" x="390" y="355">diagnostics decide whether the inverse map is credible</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The deck is not trying to sell dynamic choice. It is specifying the inverse problem.
:::

---

## Contract with the audience

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">STYLE</span>
This is a formal map, not a proof. Each slide states the object, the algorithm, or the obstruction.
:::

::: {.twocol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">We will state</span>
- model objects
- assumptions
- objective functions
- algorithm inputs and outputs
- diagnostics and counterexamples
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">We will not do</span>
- derivations of fixed-point theorems
- asymptotic inference proofs
- optimizer implementation details
- benchmark theater without failure modes
:::
:::

---

## The entire diagram in one line

::: {.eqbox}
$$
D \xrightarrow{\text{estimator + assumptions}} \widehat{\theta}
\xrightarrow{\text{Bellman solve}} (\widehat{Q},\widehat{V},\widehat{\pi})
\xrightarrow{\text{primitive edit}} \pi_{cf}
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
<span class="tag">Meaning</span>
A structural counterfactual is only as good as the estimated primitive object that survives the edit.
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 245" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="60" y="70" width="140" height="70"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="130" y="112">D</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 205 105 L 320 105"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="330" y="70" width="160" height="70"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="410" y="112">theta_hat</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 495 105 L 610 105"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="620" y="70" width="190" height="70"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="715" y="112">Q,V,pi</text>
  <path class="edge amber" d="M 715 145 C 715 210 240 210 130 145"/>
  <text class="svgnote" x="422" y="220">model checks feed back into estimator choice</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Why IID choice is the wrong object

::: {.twocol}
::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">IID choice model</span>
$$
\Pr(a_t \mid x_t)
$$
The action is a response to current covariates.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Dynamic choice model</span>
$$
\Pr(a_t \mid s_t), \qquad s_{t+1}\sim P(\cdot\mid s_t,a_t)
$$
The action changes the next problem instance.
:::
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 250" class="viz">
  <circle class="node blue" cx="205" cy="120" r="44"/><text class="svgtxt" x="205" y="126">x_t</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 250 120 L 360 120"/>
  <circle class="node cyan" cx="405" cy="120" r="44"/><text class="svgtxt" x="405" y="126">a_t</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="305" y="180">static</text>
  <circle class="node green" cx="610" cy="120" r="44"/><text class="svgtxt" x="610" y="126">s_t</text>
  <path class="edge green" d="M 655 120 L 760 120"/>
  <circle class="node amber" cx="805" cy="120" r="44"/><text class="svgtxt" x="805" y="126">a_t</text>
  <path class="edge amber dash" d="M 805 76 C 780 30 635 30 612 76"/>
  <text class="svgnote" x="710" y="180">dynamic</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Definition: controlled Markov process

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Definition 1</span>
A controlled Markov process is a tuple
$$
M = (S,A,P,\beta)
$$
where $S$ is a state space, $A(s)$ is the feasible action set, $P(s'\mid s,a)$ is a transition kernel, and $\beta\in(0,1)$ discounts future payoff.
:::

::: {.meaning}
The word Markov is a compression claim: the chosen state must contain the past information needed for prediction and payoff.
:::

---

## Definition: structural reward

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Definition 2</span>
A reward specification is a parametric map
$$
r_\theta:S\times A\to R,\qquad r_\theta(s,a)=\theta^\top f(s,a)
$$
or a structured nonlinear map whose parameters retain a declared interpretation.
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Failure mode</span>
An arbitrary reward approximator can predict behavior while failing to define a stable primitive for policy edits.
:::

---

## Definition: policy and value

::: {.mathgrid}
::: {.eqbox}
$$
\pi(a\mid s)\in \Delta(A(s))
$$
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
V^\pi(s)=E_\pi\left[\sum_{t\ge 0}\beta^t r(s_t,a_t)\mid s_0=s\right]
$$
:::
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Definition 3</span>
The policy is the behavioral object. The value is the future-inclusive payoff of starting from a state.
:::

---

## The observed data object

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Data</span>
$$
D=\{(i,t,s_{it},a_{it},s_{i,t+1})\}_{i=1,\ldots,N;\ t=1,\ldots,T_i}
$$
:::

::: {.twocol}
::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Panel content</span>
- state before choice
- chosen action
- next state
- optional covariates and agent ids
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">What is not observed</span>
- private shocks
- full reward
- value function
- rejected action outcomes
:::
:::

---

## The loop that creates trajectories

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 430" class="viz tall">
  <circle class="node blue" cx="170" cy="210" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="170" y="216">s_t</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 225 210 L 335 210"/>
  <circle class="node cyan" cx="390" cy="210" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="390" y="216">a_t</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 445 210 L 555 210"/>
  <circle class="node amber" cx="610" cy="210" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="610" y="216">r_t</text>
  <path class="edge amber" d="M 665 210 L 775 210"/>
  <circle class="node green" cx="830" cy="210" r="58"/><text class="svgtxt" x="830" y="205">s_{t+1}</text>
  <path class="edge green dash" d="M 830 150 C 760 55 230 55 175 155"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel" x="280" y="315" width="440" height="54"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="500" y="349">policy and transition jointly generate the sample path</text>
</svg>
```

::: {.meaning}
This is why dynamic choice is not a classifier with a fancy loss.
:::

---

## Bellman operator

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Operator</span>
For logit shocks with scale $\sigma$:
$$
Q_\theta(s,a)=r_\theta(s,a)+\beta\sum_{s'\in S}P(s'\mid s,a)V_\theta(s')
$$
$$
V_\theta(s)=\sigma\log\sum_{b\in A(s)}\exp(Q_\theta(s,b)/\sigma)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
The fixed point turns primitives into choice probabilities.
:::

---

## Policy induced by the fixed point

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\pi_\theta(a\mid s)=
\frac{\exp(Q_\theta(s,a)/\sigma)}
{\sum_{b\in A(s)}\exp(Q_\theta(s,b)/\sigma)}
$$
:::

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Interpretation</span>
The stochastic policy is not a behavioral afterthought. It is the observable implication of the solved dynamic program.
:::

---

## Private shocks are a smoothing device

::: {.twocol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Latent utility</span>
$$
U(a,s)=Q_\theta(s,a)+\epsilon_a
$$
With extreme-value shocks, choice probabilities become softmax.
:::

::: {.meaning}
The shocks explain why identical observed states need not produce identical actions.
:::
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 230" class="viz">
  <line class="axis" x1="145" y1="145" x2="855" y2="145"/>
  <circle class="node green" cx="310" cy="145" r="34"/><text class="svgtxt small" x="310" y="150">keep</text>
  <circle class="node red" cx="690" cy="145" r="34"/><text class="svgtxt small" x="690" y="150">replace</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="500" y="205">soft choices reveal value differences, not deterministic labels</text>
</svg>
```

---

## The likelihood object

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\ell(\theta;D)=\sum_{(i,t)}\log \pi_\theta(a_{it}\mid s_{it})
$$
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Estimation claim</span>
Maximizing this likelihood is meaningful only after the state, action set, transition model, reward specification, and shock normalization have been fixed.
:::

---

## Inverse problem, not curve fitting

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Proposition 1</span>
If two parameter vectors induce the same conditional choice probabilities on the observed support, then the data cannot distinguish them without additional restrictions.
:::

::: {.meaning}
The estimator is only as formal as its identification restrictions.
:::

---

## Section marker: assumptions

::: {.sep}
<div>
<div class="kicker">from objects to claims</div>
<div class="mono">Assumptions are the load-bearing part of the talk.</div>
</div>
:::

---

## Assumption: Markov state sufficiency

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Assumption A1</span>
For all histories $h_t$ compressed to the same state $s_t$:
$$
\Pr(s_{t+1},a_t,r_t\mid h_t)=\Pr(s_{t+1},a_t,r_t\mid s_t)
$$
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Diagnostic</span>
If residual transition errors depend on omitted history, the state is not sufficient.
:::

---

## State design as compression

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 390" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="80" y="82" width="330" height="215"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="245" y="130">history</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="245" y="180">h_t=(s_0,a_0,...,s_t)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="245" y="230">too large to estimate directly</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 420 190 L 565 190"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="585" y="82" width="330" height="215"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="750" y="130">state</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="750" y="180">s_t = c(h_t)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="750" y="230">sufficient statistic if A1 holds</text>
</svg>
```

::: {.meaning}
A state is an algorithmic summary, not a spreadsheet column.
:::

---

## Assumption: support

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Assumption A2</span>
For any state-action pair used by estimation or counterfactual simulation:
$$
\Pr_D(s,a)>0 \quad\text{or}\quad (s,a)\ \text{is covered by a justified model extrapolation}
$$
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Failure</span>
CCP-style methods become unstable when the estimated policy assigns near-zero probability to actions needed for inversion.
:::

---

## Assumption: action contrast

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Assumption A3</span>
The feature matrix must vary across feasible actions:
$$
\operatorname{rank}\{f(s,a)-f(s,a_0):s\in S,\ a\in A(s)\}>0
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
Features that do not change the action comparison cannot identify choice tradeoffs.
:::

---

## Visual diagnostic: action contrast

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 390" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel red" x="80" y="70" width="360" height="230"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="260" y="118">bad feature</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="260" y="172">f(s,keep)=f(s,replace)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="260" y="225">cancels in every comparison</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="560" y="70" width="360" height="230"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="740" y="118">useful feature</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="740" y="172">f(s,keep)-f(s,replace)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="740" y="225">moves the log odds</text>
  <path class="edge red dash" d="M 442 185 L 555 185"/>
</svg>
```

---

## Assumption: primitive stability

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Counterfactual validity condition</span>
A counterfactual policy edit is interpretable only if the edited model preserves the primitive reward, transition, and choice-shock objects that were meant to remain stable.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
M_{\hat\theta}=(S,A,P,r_{\hat\theta},\beta,\sigma)
\quad\leadsto\quad
M_{cf}=(S,A_{cf},P_{cf},r_{cf},\beta_{cf},\sigma)
$$
:::

---

## Normalization is not cosmetic

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Assumption A4</span>
Utilities are identified up to normalizations. Scale and location must be fixed before comparing parameter magnitudes.
:::

::: {.mathgrid}
::: {.eqbox}
$$
r'(s,a)=r(s,a)+c
$$
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
r'(s,a)=\alpha r(s,a),\quad \sigma'=\alpha\sigma
$$
:::
:::

::: {.meaning}
Without normalization, "larger reward" can mean "different unit."
:::

---

## Counterexample: reward shaping

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Counterexample 1</span>
Potential-based shaping can preserve optimal behavior:
$$
r'(s,a,s')=r(s,a,s')+\beta\Phi(s')-\Phi(s)
$$
while changing apparent per-period rewards.
:::

::: {.meaning}
Policy equivalence is not reward uniqueness.
:::

---

## The inverse map has null spaces

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 420" class="viz tall">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="85" y="100" width="310" height="210"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="240" y="148">same policy</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 150 240 C 220 170 300 220 340 150"/>
  <circle class="node blue" cx="150" cy="240" r="16"/>
  <circle class="node blue" cx="245" cy="200" r="16"/>
  <circle class="node blue" cx="340" cy="150" r="16"/>
  <path class="edge amber" d="M 405 205 L 555 205"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel amber" x="575" y="100" width="340" height="210"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="745" y="148">many rewards</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="745" y="198">r</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="745" y="238">r + beta Phi(s') - Phi(s)</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="745" y="278">behavior unchanged</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Section marker: algorithmic estimators

::: {.sep}
<div>
<div class="kicker">from assumptions to computation</div>
<div class="mono">Every estimator chooses where to pay the fixed-point cost.</div>
</div>
:::

---

## Estimator taxonomy as a compiler choice

::: {.threecol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">NFXP</span>
Solve the dynamic program inside each likelihood evaluation.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">CCP / NPL</span>
Use estimated choice probabilities to bypass or approximate repeated solves.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">IRL</span>
Recover reward from demonstrations, often through moments, entropy, or adversarial objectives.
:::
:::

---

## Algorithm: soft value iteration

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 1</span>
```text
input: theta, P, beta, sigma, tolerance eps
initialize V_0
repeat:
    Q_k(s,a) = r_theta(s,a) + beta E[V_k(s') | s,a]
    V_{k+1}(s) = sigma log sum_a exp(Q_k(s,a)/sigma)
until ||V_{k+1} - V_k||_inf <= eps
output: V, Q, pi
```
:::

---

## Visual: Bellman contraction

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 360" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel" x="80" y="135" width="135" height="54"/><text class="svgmono" x="148" y="168">V_0</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 220 162 L 330 162"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="340" y="135" width="135" height="54"/><text class="svgmono" x="408" y="168">T V_0</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 480 162 L 590 162"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="600" y="135" width="135" height="54"/><text class="svgmono" x="668" y="168">T^2 V_0</text>
  <path class="edge green" d="M 740 162 L 850 162"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="860" y="135" width="80" height="54"/><text class="svgmono" x="900" y="168">V*</text>
  <text class="svgnote" x="500" y="250">The economics lives in r and P. The computation is repeated application of T.</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Algorithm: likelihood evaluation

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 2</span>
```text
input: theta, data D
V,Q,pi = SolveBellman(theta)
score = 0
for (s_it, a_it) in D:
    score += log pi(a_it | s_it)
output: score
```
:::

::: {.meaning}
NFXP nests Algorithm 2 inside an optimizer over $\theta$.
:::

---

## NFXP: exact structural likelihood

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Target</span>
$$
\widehat{\theta}_{NFXP}
\in\arg\max_\theta
\sum_{(i,t)}\log \pi_\theta(a_{it}\mid s_{it})
$$
subject to the Bellman fixed point that defines $\pi_\theta$.
:::

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Strength</span>
If the primitives are correctly specified and the optimizer succeeds, the counterfactual object is directly available.
:::

---

## NFXP: where the cost sits

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 390" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="80" y="100" width="210" height="90"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="185" y="153">optimizer(theta)</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 295 145 L 420 145"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="435" y="100" width="210" height="90"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="540" y="153">solve Bellman</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 650 145 L 775 145"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="790" y="100" width="130" height="90"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="855" y="153">loglik</text>
  <path class="edge red dash" d="M 855 195 C 770 310 250 310 185 195"/>
  <text class="svgnote" x="500" y="328">The inner fixed point is paid again and again.</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Canonical NFXP instance: replacement

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Rust-style model</span>
State is mileage. Actions are keep or replace. Replacement resets the state and pays a fixed cost.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
r_\theta(s,\text{keep})=-c\cdot s,
\qquad
r_\theta(s,\text{replace})=-RC
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
The model is small enough to see every moving part.
:::

---

## Evidence: observed replacement data

![](assets/rust_bus_data_profile.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Observed support and action imbalance. This is a diagnostic for whether the inverse problem is well conditioned.
:::

---

## Evidence: estimated replacement policy

![](assets/rust_bus_policies.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Policy curves are the observable implication of estimated primitives, not an end in themselves.
:::

---

## Evidence: value function

![](assets/rust_bus_values.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
The value graph checks whether the solved dynamic program has a coherent continuation-value geometry.
:::

---

## Result table: Rust-style replication

<table class="resulttable">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Object</th>
      <th>Reference</th>
      <th>EconIRL</th>
      <th>Diagnostic reading</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td>Replacement cost</td><td>large negative utility</td><td>matched scale</td><td>fixed cost recovered</td></tr>
    <tr><td>Mileage cost slope</td><td>small negative slope</td><td>matched sign</td><td>state cost recovered</td></tr>
    <tr><td>Policy shape</td><td>monotone replace</td><td>monotone replace</td><td>dynamic logic visible</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

::: {.caption}
The table is intentionally read as object recovery, not as decorative benchmarking.
:::

---

## Evidence: parameter recovery

![](assets/nfxp_recovery.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Known-truth recovery is the sanity check that the estimator is not merely fitting observed labels.
:::

---

## NFXP diagnostic checklist

::: {.fourcol}
::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">State</span>
Does $s$ predict transitions and payoff-relevant behavior?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Transition</span>
Is $P(s'\mid s,a)$ estimated on support?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Reward</span>
Do parameters have stable units?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Solve</span>
Does the fixed point converge consistently?
:::
:::

---

## NFXP failure mode

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Counterexample 2</span>
If the transition kernel is fit using post-policy information that would not exist under the counterfactual, then a clean likelihood can still produce invalid counterfactuals.
:::

::: {.meaning}
The likelihood score cannot certify the data-generating invariances by itself.
:::

---

## Benchmark: speed and fit

![](assets/rust_bus_benchmark.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Runtime is a property of the computational organization. Fit is a property of the induced policy. Both matter.
:::

---

## CCP: replace solves with observed policy

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Idea</span>
Estimate conditional choice probabilities first, then invert them to recover value differences.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\widehat{p}(a\mid s)\approx \Pr_D(a_t=a\mid s_t=s)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
CCP estimators trade dynamic solves for first-stage policy estimation and support conditions.
:::

---

## Stated inversion, not derivation

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Hotz-Miller-style statement</span>
Under logit shocks and full support, conditional choice probabilities identify choice-specific value differences up to normalization.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
Q(s,a)-Q(s,a_0)=
\sigma\left[\log p(a\mid s)-\log p(a_0\mid s)\right]
$$
:::

---

## Algorithm: CCP estimator

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 3</span>
```text
input: panel D
estimate p_hat(a | s)
estimate transition P_hat(s' | s,a)
use inversion to express value differences
estimate theta from implied choice equations
output: theta_hat, implied policy
```
:::

---

## CCP first stage as statistical object

::: {.twocol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Input</span>
Counts or smooth estimates of action frequencies by state.
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Risk</span>
Thin cells produce unstable log odds and invalid inversions.
:::
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 230" class="viz">
  <line class="axis" x1="110" y1="160" x2="890" y2="160"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="160" y="80" width="110" height="80"/><text class="svgmono" x="215" y="126">dense</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel amber" x="445" y="105" width="110" height="55"/><text class="svgmono" x="500" y="140">thin</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel red" x="730" y="132" width="110" height="28"/><text class="svgmono" x="785" y="153">zero</text>
</svg>
```

---

## CCP continuation values

::: {.eqbox}
$$
E\left[V(s_{t+1})\mid s_t=s,a_t=a\right]
=\sum_{s'}P(s'\mid s,a)V(s')
$$
:::

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Computation</span>
Even after inversion, the continuation object still enters the moment or likelihood equations.
:::

---

## CCP support diagnostic

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Bad sign</span>
A state-action pair appears in the counterfactual simulator but has no support in the observed CCP table.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\widehat{p}(a\mid s)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\log \widehat{p}(a\mid s)=-\infty
$$
:::

---

## NPL: fixed point on policies

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Nested pseudo-likelihood</span>
NPL iterates between a policy guess and a parameter update rather than solving the full dynamic program at every outer step.
:::

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 4</span>
```text
initialize pi_0
repeat:
    estimate theta_k given pi_k
    update pi_{k+1} from theta_k
until policy update is stable
```
:::

---

## MPEC: expose the fixed point

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Optimization rewrite</span>
Move the Bellman equation from an inner solver into explicit constraints:
$$
\max_{\theta,V}\ell(\theta,V;D)
\quad\text{s.t.}\quad
V=T_\theta V
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
Same mathematics, different numerical surface.
:::

---

## UFXP: update instead of fully solving

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Computation</span>
Updating-value methods let optimization progress and Bellman updates interleave.
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 300" class="viz">
  <circle class="node blue" cx="190" cy="145" r="46"/><text class="svgtxt" x="190" y="151">theta</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 238 145 L 360 145"/>
  <circle class="node cyan" cx="410" cy="145" r="46"/><text class="svgtxt" x="410" y="140">one</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="410" y="164">update</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 458 145 L 580 145"/>
  <circle class="node green" cx="630" cy="145" r="46"/><text class="svgtxt" x="630" y="140">better</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="630" y="164">V</text>
  <path class="edge red dash" d="M 630 95 C 540 35 270 35 195 95"/>
</svg>
```

---

## TD-CCP: learn continuation from transitions

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Idea</span>
Use temporal-difference structure to estimate continuation values from realized transitions.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\delta_t = r_\theta(s_t,a_t)+\beta V(s_{t+1})-V(s_t)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
This imports an RL-style value-estimation move into the CCP family.
:::

---

## Section marker: inverse reinforcement learning

::: {.sep}
<div>
<div class="kicker">from structural likelihood to demonstrations</div>
<div class="mono">IRL changes the reward language and the evidence target.</div>
</div>
:::

---

## IRL problem statement

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">IRL input</span>
Expert demonstrations:
$$
\tau=(s_0,a_0,s_1,a_1,\ldots)
$$
plus a dynamics model or simulator.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">IRL output</span>
A reward function whose induced policy explains the demonstrations.
:::

---

## Feature map is the reward language

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Feature reward</span>
$$
r_\theta(s,a)=\theta^\top f(s,a)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
IRL cannot recover preferences over distinctions that the feature map never encodes.
:::

---

## MCE-IRL objective

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Maximum causal entropy</span>
Among policies matching expert feature expectations, prefer the highest-entropy causal policy.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\max_\pi H(\pi)
\quad\text{s.t.}\quad
E_\pi\left[\sum_t f(s_t,a_t)\right]
=
E_E\left[\sum_t f(s_t,a_t)\right]
$$
:::

---

## MCE reward dual

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Statement</span>
The reward weights are dual variables for feature expectation matching.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\nabla_\theta L(\theta)=\mu_E-\mu_{\pi_\theta}
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
Learning stops when model trajectories and expert trajectories agree in feature space.
:::

---

## Algorithm: MCE-IRL

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 5</span>
```text
input: expert trajectories, features f, dynamics P
initialize theta
repeat:
    solve soft dynamic program for pi_theta
    estimate model feature counts mu_pi
    theta <- theta + eta (mu_E - mu_pi)
until feature mismatch is small
output: reward weights theta
```
:::

---

## MCE as a moment-matching diagram

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 380" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="90" y="90" width="260" height="160"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="220" y="136">expert</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="220" y="185">mu_E</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="650" y="90" width="260" height="160"/>
  <text class="svgtxt big" x="780" y="136">model</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="780" y="185">mu_pi(theta)</text>
  <path class="edge amber" d="M 360 170 L 640 170"/>
  <text class="svgnote" x="500" y="150">match feature counts</text>
  <text class="svgmono" x="500" y="260">gradient = mu_E - mu_pi(theta)</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Canonical MCE example: paths on a grid

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">State and action</span>
States are grid cells. Actions move the agent. Features encode goal proximity, walls, terrain, or risk.
:::

::: {.meaning}
The example is small, but it exposes the identifiability issue: rewards are learned only through demonstrated alternatives.
:::

---

## Evidence: taxi gridworld IRL

![](assets/taxi_gridworld_results.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Reward learning is evaluated by induced behavior and transfer, not by whether a reward heatmap looks plausible.
:::

---

## MCE failure mode

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Counterexample 3</span>
Two different rewards that agree on the chosen feature expectations can be indistinguishable to MCE-IRL.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\mu_E=\mu_{\pi_{\theta_1}}=\mu_{\pi_{\theta_2}}
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
r_{\theta_1}=r_{\theta_2}
$$
:::

---

## AIRL discriminator

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Structured discriminator</span>
$$
D_\psi(s,a,s')=
\frac{\exp(f_\psi(s,a,s'))}
{\exp(f_\psi(s,a,s'))+\pi(a\mid s)}
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
AIRL is not just a classifier. Its discriminator is structured to separate reward-like terms from shaping terms.
:::

---

## AIRL decomposition

::: {.eqbox}
$$
f_\psi(s,a,s')=g_\psi(s,a)+\beta h_\psi(s')-h_\psi(s)
$$
:::

::: {.twocol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">g term</span>
Reward-like component.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">h term</span>
Potential function that absorbs shaping.
:::
:::

---

## Algorithm: AIRL

::: {.algobox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm 6</span>
```text
input: expert trajectories, simulator
initialize policy pi and discriminator D
repeat:
    sample learner trajectories from pi
    train D to separate expert from learner
    extract reward signal from D
    update pi with RL
until policy and discriminator stabilize
output: reward-like function and policy
```
:::

---

## AIRL training loop

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 395" class="viz">
  <circle class="node blue" cx="190" cy="200" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="190" y="194">expert</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="190" y="220">data</text>
  <circle class="node cyan" cx="500" cy="95" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="500" y="89">policy</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="500" y="115">rollouts</text>
  <circle class="node amber" cx="810" cy="200" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="810" y="194">disc</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="810" y="220">classify</text>
  <circle class="node green" cx="500" cy="310" r="54"/><text class="svgtxt" x="500" y="304">reward</text><text class="svgtxt small" x="500" y="330">signal</text>
  <path class="edge blue" d="M 244 190 C 390 150 610 150 756 190"/>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 545 124 C 655 140 735 160 770 177"/>
  <path class="edge amber" d="M 775 232 C 700 290 610 312 554 312"/>
  <path class="edge green" d="M 454 286 C 380 250 300 220 245 205"/>
</svg>
```

---

## State-only transfer claim

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">AIRL-style transfer statement</span>
If the recovered reward is state-only and the shaping term is separated, then reward can be more stable under dynamics changes than a policy imitation objective.
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Caveat</span>
This is a conditional statement. It is not a universal reward-identification theorem.
:::

---

## Action-dependent economics

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Structural tension</span>
Economic primitives often live on actions: replacement cost, entry cost, switching cost, search cost.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
r(s,a)=r_{state}(s)+r_{action}(a)+r_{interaction}(s,a)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
Action-dependent rewards are useful, but they weaken the clean state-only transfer story.
:::

---

## AIRL failure mode

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Counterexample 4</span>
If demonstrations do not include alternative dynamics or enough action support, AIRL can learn a reward-like discriminator signal that transfers poorly outside the training environment.
:::

::: {.meaning}
Adversarial training does not remove the support problem.
:::

---

## Neural surrogates

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Purpose</span>
Approximate a hard object:
$$
V_\phi(s),\quad Q_\phi(s,a),\quad r_\phi(s,a)
$$
when tabular state spaces or linear rewards are too small.
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Risk</span>
Approximation can hide identification failures behind predictive fit.
:::

---

## Heterogeneity

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Latent type model</span>
$$
\pi(a\mid s)=\sum_{k=1}^K w_k(s)\pi_k(a\mid s)
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
One average policy may be an artifact of several distinct decision rules.
:::

---

## Bounded planning horizons

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Finite horizon approximation</span>
$$
V_H(s)=\max_\pi E_\pi\left[\sum_{t=0}^{H}\beta^t r(s_t,a_t)\mid s_0=s\right]
$$
:::

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Modeling interpretation</span>
The horizon can be a computational approximation or a behavioral claim about limited planning.
:::

---

## Evidence: estimator simulation study

![](assets/rust_bus_results.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Simulation-study evidence checks known-truth recovery across estimator families. Treat it as validation evidence, not as a leaderboard.
:::

---

## Known truth versus replication

::: {.twocol}
::: {.evidence}
<span class="tag">Known truth</span>
Simulation lets us ask whether the estimator recovers the data-generating parameter.
:::

::: {.evidence}
<span class="tag">Replication</span>
Empirical replication asks whether a model reproduces published or observed behavior.
:::
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Do not conflate</span>
A clean replication plot is not proof of identification.
:::

---

## Section marker: counterfactuals

::: {.sep}
<div>
<div class="kicker">from estimation to interventions</div>
<div class="mono">A counterfactual is a solved model after a primitive edit.</div>
</div>
:::

---

## Counterfactual operator

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Definition 4</span>
A counterfactual operator edits primitives and re-solves:
$$
C_g(M_{\hat\theta}) = \operatorname{SolveBellman}(g(M_{\hat\theta}))
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
This is not a label swap and not a prediction from the old policy.
:::

---

## Counterfactual graph: replacement cost

![](assets/rust_bus_counterfactual.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
Changing the replacement-cost primitive changes the solved policy curve. The plot is evidence about the model's implied intervention response.
:::

---

## Counterfactual graph: elasticity

![](assets/rust_bus_elasticity.png){.figimg}

::: {.caption}
The response curve summarizes a family of solved counterfactual policies.
:::

---

## Proposition: counterfactual validity

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Proposition 2</span>
If the estimated primitives are identified on the relevant support and the counterfactual edit preserves the invariances assumed by the model, then the solved counterfactual policy is interpretable as a model-implied intervention.
:::

::: {.counterbox}
<span class="tag">Negation</span>
If support, invariance, or state sufficiency fails, the counterfactual can be precise and wrong.
:::

---

## Counterfactual audit checklist

::: {.fourcol}
::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Support</span>
Are edited states and actions covered?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Invariant</span>
Which primitive is held fixed?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Solve</span>
Was the model re-solved?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Report</span>
Is uncertainty separated from policy response?
:::
:::

---

## Section marker: complexity and selection

::: {.sep}
<div>
<div class="kicker">choosing the method</div>
<div class="mono">The right estimator is the one whose assumptions match the failure mode.</div>
</div>
:::

---

## Complexity surface

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\text{total cost}
\approx
(\text{outer optimizer steps})
\times
(\text{Bellman solve cost})
\times
(\text{state-action size})
$$
:::

::: {.claimbox}
<span class="tag">Method choice</span>
NFXP pays in solves. CCP pays in support and first-stage error. Neural IRL pays in approximation and audit difficulty.
:::

---

## Estimator tradeoff table

<table class="resulttable">
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Method</th>
      <th>Object targeted</th>
      <th>Computational shortcut</th>
      <th>Main audit</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td>NFXP</td><td>structural theta</td><td>none</td><td>inner solve and specification</td></tr>
    <tr><td>CCP</td><td>same theta</td><td>invert observed policy</td><td>support and first-stage error</td></tr>
    <tr><td>MCE-IRL</td><td>feature reward</td><td>moment matching</td><td>feature completeness</td></tr>
    <tr><td>AIRL</td><td>reward-like signal</td><td>adversarial split</td><td>shaping and transfer scope</td></tr>
    <tr><td>Neural variants</td><td>large-state approximations</td><td>function approximation</td><td>extrapolation and calibration</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

---

## When to use which family

::: {.threecol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Use NFXP</span>
when the state space is small enough and primitive interpretation is central.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Use CCP/NPL</span>
when support is rich and repeated full solves are too expensive.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Use IRL</span>
when demonstrations and feature reward recovery are the natural evidence objects.
:::
:::

---

## What the CS theorist should ask

::: {.stack}
::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Identifiability</span>
What equivalence class of rewards or primitives is actually identified?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Complexity</span>
Where is the fixed-point cost paid, approximated, or avoided?
:::

::: {.assumbox}
<span class="tag">Robustness</span>
Which support or invariance violation breaks the counterfactual?
:::
:::

---

## Where this lives in EconIRL

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Implementation map</span>
Core dynamic programming defines the operator. Estimators call it, approximate it, or invert around it.
:::

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 1000 360" class="viz">
  <rect class="svgpanel cyan" x="210" y="55" width="580" height="62"/>
  <text class="svgmono" x="500" y="93">soft Bellman operator</text>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 500 120 L 250 185"/>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 500 120 L 500 185"/>
  <path class="edge cyan" d="M 500 120 L 750 185"/>
  <rect class="svgpanel blue" x="110" y="195" width="280" height="58"/><text class="svgmono" x="250" y="230">NFXP / structural likelihood</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel amber" x="360" y="195" width="280" height="58"/><text class="svgmono" x="500" y="230">CCP / NPL shortcuts</text>
  <rect class="svgpanel green" x="610" y="195" width="280" height="58"/><text class="svgmono" x="750" y="230">MCE / AIRL reward recovery</text>
</svg>
```

---

## Minimal example every estimator should pass

::: {.propbox}
<span class="tag">Sanity test</span>
On a small synthetic MDP with known reward and transition kernel, the estimator should recover the induced policy and the identifiable reward object.
:::

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\text{known }(r,P,\beta)
\rightarrow
D
\rightarrow
\widehat{r},\widehat{\pi}
\approx
r,\pi
$$
:::

---

## What a theorem would have to specify

::: {.fourcol}
::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Class</span>
State, action, reward, transition families.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Equivalence</span>
Reward normalizations and shaping.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Sample</span>
Support and concentration conditions.
:::

::: {.defbox}
<span class="tag">Algorithm</span>
Approximation and optimization error.
:::
:::

---

## What an empirical plot must show

::: {.stack}
::: {.evidence}
<span class="tag">First</span>
The data support relevant state-action comparisons.
:::

::: {.evidence}
<span class="tag">Second</span>
The solved model reproduces observed behavior on support.
:::

::: {.evidence}
<span class="tag">Third</span>
The counterfactual is a primitive edit followed by a re-solve.
:::
:::

---

## Final mental model

::: {.eqbox}
$$
\text{dynamic choice}
=
\text{controlled Markov process}
+
\text{inverse problem}
+
\text{counterfactual operator}
$$
:::

::: {.meaning}
The point is not to make choice theory look like RL. The point is to expose the common operator and then ask which assumptions make the inverse map meaningful.
:::

---

## Reference anchors

::: {.small}
- Rust (1987): nested fixed-point estimation for replacement.
- Hotz and Miller (1993): conditional choice probability inversion.
- Aguirregabiria and Mira (2010): dynamic discrete choice survey spine.
- Ziebart (2010): maximum causal entropy IRL.
- Fu, Luo, and Levine (2018): AIRL and reward shaping structure.
- EconIRL examples and simulation artifacts: figures shown in this deck.
:::

::: {.meaning}
Use these as anchors for the formal objects, not as appeals to authority.
:::
